---
title: "Introduction to Applied AI Bootcamp"
format: revealjs
---

![](../assets/program_info.png){fig-align="center"}


## Schedule

::: {.incremental}

- **Course 1: Python**  
    Build strong Python programming foundations to apply and integrate ML/DL/AI in software systems.
- **Course 2: Applied Data Science**  
    Calculate, analyze, visualize, interpret data and test hypothesis using Data Science.
- **Course 3: Applied Machine Learning**  
    Build reliable predictive modeling pipelines, debug its issues, evaluate and compare alternatives.
- **Course 4: Applied Deep Learning**  
    Use DL solution frameworks / libraries to apply AI to specific problems in vision and language.
- **Course 5: Agentic AI**  
    Develop, debug, evaluate deploy, and monitor LLM-driven AI Agents to automate tasks involving unstructured data.
- **Course 6: Agentic Engineering**  
    Use today's best practices to accelerate software development in your codebase. Previously known as *Vibe Coding*.
- **Capstone Project**  
    Build agentic AI solutions to solve problems special to AI; utilizing skills learned in the bootcamp.

:::

## Policy: Attendance

The fourth absence will result in explusion from the bootcamp:

- Being late counts as 1/4 absence
- Leaving class frequently will result in 1 absence
- Execused absences must be proved by hospital


## Pedagogy

You will do most of the work.

![Figure: Benjamin Franklin quote](../assets/tell_me_and_I_forget.png){fig-align="center"}

## Learning Components

1. Slides
2. Lecture Notes
3. Labs
4. Exercises
5. Quizzes
6. Projects

## 1. Lecture Notes

- The detailed learning material in written format.
- Spend enough focused reading time to read them to completion.
- **When?**: Read before (and after) class


## 2. Slides

- Facilitate instructors' lesson delivery.
- Press `S` to open up Speaker Notes for more details.
- **When?**: listten-in carefully to instructor in class.


## 3. Labs

- Live documents of worked-out examples
- "What if?" ask yourself, modify parts of the code cells and re-run them to verify understanding.
- **When?**: to be done in class.


## 4. Exercises

- Assignments with increasing level of difficulty
- Some will test **knowledge**. Others will test **skills** (programming)
- You are expected to finish them in class, and before the next day
- **When?**: to be done in class.


## Submit your Work

Your GitHub repo is your **proof-of-work**. Open the repo [HassanAlgoz/python](https://github.com/HassanAlgoz/python/tree/main) then:

::: {.incremental}

1. Fork it from the GitHub website
2. Clone it using VS Code locally
3. Create a `student/` folder in which you will include:
    - A `student/README.md` file with your full name in there
    - **Labs**
    - **Exercises**
    - **Projects**

:::

## Commits

1. First, `git add` and `git commit` the lab/exercise before solving it.
2. Each subsequent commit must have
    1. Week number
    2. Module number
    3. Exercise number or name
    4. Question number or title

## Commit Examples

::: {.incremental}

- **Labs**:
    1. First commit: `"W1 M2 Lab1: init"` for week 1, module 2, lab 1
    2. Second commit: `"W1 M2 Lab1: done"` to indicate you completed it
- **Execises**:
    1. `"W1 M2 Ex1: init"`
    2. `"W1 M2 Ex1: q1"`
    2. `"W1 M2 Ex1: q2"`
    2. `"W1 M2 Ex2: init"`
- **Projects**:
    1. `"W1 Project: init"`
    2. `"W1 Project: step 1. univariate analysis"`
    3. `"W1 Project: step 2. pairplots"`
::: 

## Push

::: {.incremental}
- Labs must be **pushed daily** (due start of tomorrow's first session)
- Exercises must be **pushed by Thursday** (due end of course)
- Projects must be **pushed by Sunday** (due start of next course)
- Commit history is analyzed to detect cheating
- Zero points for grouping everything into one big commit
::: 

## Evaluation

Per course:

- Quiz
- Project

## 5. Quizzes

Test knowledge and skills based on learning outcomes.

- Mostly questions from current course
- **Bonus**: Some questions from previous courses
- **When?**: Thursdays (end of course)

## 6. Projects

Apply learning to new situations.

Each project will be different depending on the course.

## Presentation and Feedback

After completing the project, prepare a classroom **Presentation** on Sunday (before start of next course).

::: {.incremental}
- What goes into the presentation depends on the project
- **Completeness**: points will be deducted if you missed requirements
- **Competency**: points will be deducted if you lack understanding
    1. you express ideas accurately in your own words (memorization + understanding)
    2. your answer matches the level of detail required by the question (stay within topic)
    3. you know when to say "I don't know" (awareness of knowledge/skill gaps)
:::

## Why Presentations?

::: {.incremental}
- Your success in life depends on three key elements:
    1. Your ability to **speak**
    2. Your ability to **write**
    3. The **quality of your ideas**
- .. in that order (as [Professor Winston puts it](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Unzc731iCUY)).
- Your ability to communicate ideas is ultimately more critical to your success than the ideas themselves.
- We want you to build this skill throuought the bootcamp
:::


## Learning and Desirable Difficulty

Your brain wants comfort; but as we all know- No pain. No gain. This is true for learning as it is true for exercising.

::: {.incremental}
- No one is perfect. Fear of mistakes is a psychological barrier to growth and exceeding your limits.
- Make as much mistakes as you can; to get as much feedback as you can while you are in class.
- Get feedback and check your assumptions and understanding; before you "move on".
- Getting help from others is good; but be sure *YOU* get it.
::: 

## Policy: Getting Help from AI

::: {.incremental}
- Relying on AI to solve your issues is **provably destructive to your learning**.
- Making mistakes is totally fine, but **cheating will result in banning from the bootcamp** without the possibility of return. This will be taken very seriously and no exceptions will be given whatsoever.
- We have sophisticated ways of detection.
- Instructors are here to help you; don't be afraid to ask questions you feel are "stupid" because there is no such thing.
::: 

## Bad Students

::: {.incremental}
1. Let AI handle everything
2. Use someone else's work
3. Skip content and not put in any effort
4. Unaware of required work to be done
5. Don't commit as per the instructions
::: 

## Good Students

::: {.incremental}
1. You are honest and consistent in putting in the required effort in class
2. You pay attention to instructions and ask when things aren't clear
3. You ask the right questions; for the sake of learning
4. You are not afraid of making mistakes; for the sake of exceeding your limits
:::
